latest.title: FY27 Quarter 1 results
latest.content:
Today 07:00
RNS Number : 3321M
Zegona Communications PLC
15 July 2026
FY27 Quarter 1 results
15 July 2026
LEI: 213800ASI1VZL2ED4S65
FY27 Quarter 1 results
A solid foundation for future growth
Zegona announces Vodafone Spain and Zegona Holdco Limited's results for Q1 FY27. These results delivered a third consecutive quarter of top line growth and continued the positive trends reported in FY26.
Since the acquisition of Vodafone Spain two years ago, our strategy has focused on customer investment, driving operational efficiencies and growing cashflow. This has led to another strong quarter with revenue growth, increased EBITDAaL, improved cashflow, with lower net debt and leverage.
At the end of the quarter, broadband lines reached 2,592k growing 22k year on year and mobile lines reached 12,864k growing 345k. Total revenues grew 2% year on year to €916m, EBITDA grew 5% to €465m and EBITDAaL grew 3% to €324m. This demonstrates the continued success of investing in our customers, our multi-brand strategy and improved customer propositions.
The operating cashflow for the quarter was €204m with margins of over 22%. We also reduced net debt by 11% to €3.2b and decreased leverage to 2.33x.
In June 2026 we announced the successful refinancing of all existing senior secured notes and senior facilities. This transaction will deliver annual interest savings of c.€60m1 and extends the maturity of Zegona's capital structure beyond five years. The €3.7b refinancing further demonstrates global credit investor support for Zegona's strategy. Two years ago, the Company's annual interest cost was €294m, at March 2026 it was €230m, and going forwards it will be only €170m2. The refinancing closed on 14 July 20263.
Operational KPIs (EoP 000s)
Q1 FY26
Q1 FY27

Notes:
1. Annual interest savings of c.€60m is an estimated run rate saving post the refinancing completion. This rate is calculated using Euribor which can change.
2. Run rate cost of debt annualised. Cost of debt includes debt interest minus interest on cash. Cost of debt can change with market movements in Euribor.
3. The refinancing was priced on 25 June 2026, and closing occurred on 14 July 2026. The previous notes will be redeemed on 15 July 2026.
4. Includes both contract and prepay mobile lines. Mobile contract lines have increased by 126k to 10,222k in Q1 FY27, compared to 10,096k in Q1 FY26.
5. EBITDA is defined as net loss before income tax, net financing costs, amortization of customer-related intangible assets, amortization of owned assets and depreciation of owned assets, excluding gains/losses on disposal of owned and leased assets, impairment of equity accounted investment, share of profit or loss in an associated undertaking or in a joint arrangement, separately reported items and lease costs. This is reported at the Vodafone Spain level.
6. EBITDAaL and Operating cashflow are reported at the Vodafone Spain level and defined in the Zegona Communications plc FY26 Annual Report.
7. Net debt is reported at the Zegona Holdco Limited group level and is the nominal amount of debt (with the SSN USD amounts presented as the hedged EUR value of the nominal amount) net of cash held at this level.
8. The leverage ratio is as presented in the investor presentations; Net debt divided by the trailing two quarters annualized Vodafone Spain EBITDAaL.
9. Q1 FY27 Zegona Holdco Group EBITDAaL was €323m and net cash held was €534m.
Purpose of this information
This Condensed Consolidated Financial Information satisfies Zegona's external debt reporting obligations as required pursuant to the Senior Facilities Agreement dated 15 July 2024 (as amended). Zegona HoldCo Limited and its subsidiaries ("the Zegona HoldCo Group" or "the Group") presents the Group's unaudited condensed consolidated financial information for the periods noted in the preceding pages.
For further information, please contact:

Tilly Abraham (Sodali & Co)

About Zegona
Zegona is publicly listed on the Main Market of the LSE. It was established in 2015 with the objective of investing in businesses in the European Telecommunications, Media and Technology sector and improving their performance to deliver attractive shareholder returns.
Zegona is led by former Virgin Media executives Eamonn O'Hare and Robert Samuelson. In 2024, Zegona completed the acquisition of Vodafone Spain.

company_30s.what_they_do: Zegona is a telecom‑infrastructure provider that owns and operates fibre‑optic networks across Spain.  It offers wholesale network services to mobile operators, ISPs and large enterprises, and it also develops new broadband projects in underserved regions.
company_30s.why_they_matter: Spain’s push toward gigabit connectivity and the rollout of 5G creates a large, growing market for fibre infrastructure.  Zegona’s network sits in key urban and rural corridors, giving it a competitive edge in a sector where few firms can scale quickly.
company_30s.current_state: The company has been restructuring its capital structure: it recently cancelled 69 % of its shares, cut debt through a €3.7 bn refinancing that saves €60 m in interest, and is paying a dividend that will be distributed in January.  Cash flow is still tight, but the refinancing and dividend signal a shift toward financial stability.

rns_narrative: Zegona’s latest filings show a major share‑cancellation that trimmed its equity base, a refinancing that reduces its interest burden, and a dividend that will be paid to shareholders.  These moves are aimed at cleaning up the balance sheet and returning value to investors.
The company’s directors are now holding more shares, and the board has executed several transactions in its own stock, signalling confidence in the future value of the business.
Overall, the narrative is one of a company that has survived a sharp fall, is now cutting debt, and is looking to reward shareholders while positioning itself for new network contracts.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet optimism around the dividend and debt cleanup
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Bulls view the dividend as a sign that the business can finally generate cash, and the share‑cancellation as a move that will lift the stock if the market believes the fundamentals are solid.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears point to the company’s high debt load, the fact that it is still in a cash‑burn phase, and the risk that new network contracts may not materialise quickly enough to cover the remaining liabilities.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The chatter has moved from neutral to mildly bullish after the dividend announcement, but the overall volume remains low, indicating that most traders are still watching.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends for Zegona is flat, showing no recent spikes in public interest – a classic sign that the market is sleeping on a potential surprise.

near_term_catalysts: The scheduled dividend payment on 9 January – a price bump is likely if traders buy in anticipation.
Completion of the share‑cancellation and the finalisation of the refinancing – a clear reduction in debt could lift confidence.
Any new contract announcements with major telecom operators, especially if they involve large fibre‑deployment projects.
A surge in trading volume that exceeds 1.5× the 20‑day average, signalling institutional interest.
expect_next.watch_for: Key signals to confirm the thesis are: (1) a breakout above the 30‑day moving average, (2) a volume spike that shows a new accumulation wave, and (3) a positive earnings surprise in the next quarterly report.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock appears to be in the early stages of a post‑crash recovery – the share‑cancellation has just finished and the refinancing is in place.  If the APEX score is above 8 (tight compression), a mean‑reversion bounce could be imminent, but the company still needs to prove that it can generate cash flow consistently.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: If the share price recovers to roughly the midpoint of its 52‑week range, the upside is about 70 % from the current level, matching the historic average for deep‑crash setups.  The dividend and debt reduction give the stock a new floor, while the network’s strategic position offers upside if new contracts come through.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The biggest risk is that the company’s cash burn continues, new contracts fall through, or the debt load remains unsustainable.  In that scenario the stock could slide another 30 %‑40 % before hitting a new bottom.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential loss: up to 70 % if the company fails to stabilize.  Potential gain: 70 % or more if the market rallies to the 52‑week midpoint.  The asymmetry – a small downside for a large upside – makes it attractive for a contrarian stance.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter after the stock closes above the 30‑day moving average on a day of volume at least 1.5× the 20‑day average.  Size the position so that a 10 % drop would wipe out the trade, and set a stop just below the 30‑day line to protect against a false breakout.
playbook.if_watching: Wait for the dividend payout date and the announcement of any new network contracts.  If the price moves above the 20‑day high with volume >1.2× the average, consider a small position to test the new support level.
playbook.if_passing: If the company announces a new debt default, a regulatory ban on its operations, or a sudden wipe‑out of the dividend, exit immediately – these would be terminal risks.

ai_insights.bottom_line: Zegona’s deep fall, coupled with a fresh dividend and debt cleanup, creates a high‑risk, high‑reward contrarian play that could deliver upside if the market rallies after the share‑cancellation and refinancing.

panic_analysis.opportunity_reason: 📊 Moderate fear detected - monitor for escalation

fear_vs_facts.verdict: ⚖️ Mixed signals - Fear and facts balanced, wait for clarity
